pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Black rot is caused by the fungus Diplodia seriata (syn Botryosphaeria obtusa ).

type_of_disease.quote: This fungal disease causes leaf spot, fruit rot and cankers on branches.

value: Foliar
Stem
affected_parts.quote: attacking the fruit, leaves, and bark of apple trees and other pomaceous plants

summary.value: Black rot causes large brown rotten areas on fruit (often with concentric rings), 'frog-eye' leaf spots with purplish/reddish edges and tan interiors, and sunken reddish-brown cankers on branches.
summary.quote: Large brown rotten areas can form anywhere on the fruit but are most common on the blossom end. Brown to black concentric rings can often be seen on larger infections... Infected leaves develop "frog-eye leaf spot."The se are circular spots with purplish or reddish edges and light tan interiors... Cankers appear as a sunken, reddish-brown area on infected branches.

diagnostic_features.value: There is usually one spot per fruit (distinguishing it from bitter rot); leaf lesions show purple margins with brown/tan centers giving a 'frogeye' appearance; decayed flesh is not cone-shaped (unlike bitter rot); concentric bands of mahogany brown to black alternating with brown bands form on rotted fruit; small black pycnidia appear in the centers of leaf lesions.
diagnostic_features.quote: There is usually one spot per fruit, a characteristic that distinguishes black rot from bitter rot. In addition, when cutting through the lesion, the decayed flesh will not be in the shape of a cone (like bitter rot).

value: bitter rot
look_alikes.quote: There is usually one spot per fruit, a characteristic that distinguishes black rot from bitter rot.

value: Practice good sanitation: prune out dead or diseased branches, pick all dried and shriveled (mummified) fruit remaining on the trees, and remove infected plant material from the area (burn, bury, or send to municipal composting).
Prune correctly during dormancy (below 32 F; Feb-Mar in Minnesota); first prune out all dead branches, then prune to shape the tree.
Prune and remove cankers at least 15 inches below the basal end; properly dispose of prunings by burial or burning.
Take good care of trees: choose an appropriate site, plant only hardy cultivars, provide adequate water, and maintain tree health to prevent stress.
Control fire blight by pruning out infected wood or controlling insect vectors; remove limbs or trees killed by fire blight to discourage black rot.
Fungicide sprays are usually not needed in Minnesota; Captan and sulfur products labeled for scab also control black rot.
Captan and fungicides containing a strobilurin (FRAC Group 11) as an active ingredient are effective in controlling black rot on fruit.
treatments.quote: Practice good sanitation Reduce sources of spores for future infections: Prune out dead or diseased branches. Pick all dried and shriveled fruits remaining on the trees. Remove infected plant material from the area. All infected plant parts should be burned, buried or sent to a municipal composting site... Captan and sulfur products are labeled for control of both scab and black rot.

conflicts.alternative_value: Black Rot and Frogeye Leaf Spot
conflicts.note: Penn State combines the two names because the same fungus (Diplodia seriata) causes both the fruit/branch rot phase and the frogeye leaf spot phase.
Both sources cite Botryosphaeria obtusa as a synonym; canonical form uses the current accepted name.

deltas.canonical_says: 'frog-eye' leaf spots with purplish/reddish edges and tan interiors
deltas.image_shows: irregular shapes
deltas.image_quote: The lesions are scattered across the leaf surfaces.
deltas.reasoning: Extension sources describe frogeye lesions as round to irregularly lobed, which corroborates the candidate's 'irregular shapes' framing but only partially since canonical lesions are predominantly circular.

web_support.quote: The round to irregularly lobed spots develop a light brown-to-gray center surrounded by one or more dark-brown concentric rings and a purple margin giving it a 'frogeye' appearance.
lesions are circular, tan to brown with distinct purple margins

deltas.image_shows: smooth texture

small purple flecks on leaves, which expand to 1/8"-1/4" lesions with occasional purple margins and tan/brown centers

web_support.quote: Cankers appear as a sunken, reddish-brown area on infected branches and often have rough or cracked bark.
Cankers may appear as a superficial roughening of the bark; or the bark may be killed and conspicuously cracked, especially at the margins.
Old cankers are dry and appear blistered, peeling and revealing black pimple-like, spore-carrying structures.

web_support.quote: As symptoms progress, the wood will shrink and become black with cracked edges.
Black rot cankers show dry, blistery and peeling lesions revealing black, pimple-like, spore-carrying structures.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Apple scab is caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis .

type_of_disease.quote: Apple scab is caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis .

value: Foliar
Pod
affected_parts.quote: Both the leaves and fruit of apple trees can be affected by this disease.

summary.value: Lesions appear on leaves and fruit as light green circular spots that become olive green and velvety, later darkening and becoming scabby; severe infection causes leaf curling, puckering, and premature drop, and deformed cracked fruit.
summary.quote: Lesions on the leaf first appear as light green circular spots that later become olive green and velvety in appearance... Symptoms on the fruit appear as distinct, almost circular lesions that are olive-green and velvety at first, but later become darker, scabby, and sometimes cracked.

diagnostic_features.value: Small, velvety, olive green lesions with unclear margins on the lower leaf surface that age into darker spots with more distinct margins; lesions may appear more numerous closer to the mid-vein of the leaf; on some crabapples, infections may be reddish in color.
diagnostic_features.quote: At first, the lesions are small, velvety, olive green in color, and have unclear margins (Figure 1ÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â€šÂ¬Ã¢â‚¬Å“left). On some crabapples, infections may be reddish in color.

value: Plant resistant or scab-immune apple varieties (e.g., Redfree, Prima, Liberty, Freedom, Jonafree, Macfree, Sir Prize, William's Pride, Enterprise, Gold Rush, Pristine) as the ideal method for managing scab.
Rake and destroy fallen leaves below apple and crabapple trees in the fall (or chop leaves with a mulching/flail mower).
Couple leaf chopping with two or three applications of 5 percent urea to fall foliage to speed leaf decomposition.
For new plantings, select a site with at least eight hours of direct sun and space trees so air moves easily through the canopies and orchard.
Prune trees yearly to open the canopy, increase air movement and sunlight penetration, and promote leaf drying.
Avoid overhead irrigation.
Where resistance is absent, apply protectant fungicides starting at green tip (first susceptible tissue) and continue on a 7- to 10-day schedule through petal fall and fruit development.
Use curative-action fungicides when a weather monitoring system identifies that an infection period has occurred.
Apply cover sprays during the summer only if primary-season scab control was inadequate.
Organic and backyard growers can use biocontrol products to suppress disease development, combined with resistant varieties for maximum control.
treatments.quote: Sanitation is an effective management tool for apple scab. Rake and destroy all leaves in the fall (or chop leaves with a mower). Application of urea to trees after harvest, but just prior to fall leaf drop helps to speed up leaf decomposition. Prune trees regularly to increase air movement and sunlight penetration into the canopy, which speeds up drying of the leaves and fruit, thus reducing infection. Avoid overhead irrigation. Use resistant varieties ( Redfree, Prima, Liberty, Freedom, Jonafree, Macfree, Sir Prize, WilliamÃƒÂ¢Ã¢â€šÂ¬Ã¢â€žÂ¢s Pride, Enterprise, Gold Rush , and Pristine ). Chemical Control - Apply protectant fungicides at green tip...

web_support.quote: Black, scabby spots of various sizes appear on fruits and often cause them to be dwarfed, cracked, and/or misshapen. In severe cases, apple trees severely infected with scab can be completely defoliated and the fruits reduced to distorted, blemished 'nubbins.'
As these lesions mature, they become dark brown to black, scabby, and can cause the underlying fruit tissue to become corky and cracked.

deltas.canonical_says: Small, velvety, olive green lesions with unclear margins on the lower leaf surface that age into darker spots with more distinct margins; lesions may appear more numerous closer to the mid-vein of the leaf; on some crabapples, infections may be reddish in color.
deltas.image_shows: There are no visible sporulation signs such as mycelium, spore masses, or pycnidia/fruiting bodies.
deltas.image_quote: The fruit shows signs of decay, with concentric brown patches.

dropped_contradictory.canonical_says: Lesions appear on leaves and fruit as light green circular spots that become olive green and velvety, later darkening and becoming scabby; severe infection causes leaf curling, puckering, and premature drop, and deformed cracked fruit.

dropped_contradictory.image_quote: The fruit is red with scabby lesions.
dropped_contradictory.reasoning: Concentric brown patches are diagnostic of Black Rot (Botryosphaeria obtusa), not apple scab; Venturia inaequalis produces scabby, corky, non-concentric lesions, so the observation conflicts with the canonical pathology.

web_support.quote: Apple scab caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis first appears as olive-green or dark, velvety patches on the skin... lesions progress to bare, brown and corky spots.

web_support.quote: The first sign of the disease is seen on leaves as small olive-green to dark brown spots which are generally circular and highly raised with a velvety texture. Older leaf lesions are typically raised, with a corresponding cupping on the underside of the leaf.

web_support.quote: Conidiophores emerge from subcuticular stromata within infected host tissues... producing conidia in chains that contribute to the velvety appearance of scab lesions.
Venturia inaequalis is an ascomycete fungus that produces sexual spores (ascospores) in a sac-like structure called an ascus.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cedar-apple rust is a common plant disease caused by the fungal pathogen Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae .

type_of_disease.quote: Cedar-apple rust is a common plant disease caused by the fungal pathogen Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae .

value: Foliar
Stem
Pod
affected_parts.quote: galls begin to form on the branches of the primary host ... Initial symptoms of fungal infection appear on apple and crabapple plants as small chlorotic (yellow) spots ... On the pome fruits, symptoms begin as small yellow or orange lesions

summary.value: On redcedar/juniper, dark brown swollen galls (up to two inches diameter) with circular dimples form on branches and produce bright orange gelatinous telial horns in spring. On apple/crabapple, small chlorotic yellow spots develop on leaves that turn orange with red rings, bearing orange pustules above and hairy tube-like aecia below; fruit develops yellow/orange lesions that turn brown with tube-like aecia.
summary.quote: Eventually, these galls grow and become dark brown with a diameter of up to two inches. The galls, which are not always spherical and can have lumpy areas, always have small circular dimples across the surface ... large, gelatinous protrusions will erupt from the dimples along the surface of the gall ... The protrusions are typically bright orange ... Initial symptoms of fungal infection appear on apple and crabapple plants as small chlorotic (yellow) spots, which eventually become orange and develop red rings around the edge ... fungal fruiting bodies, called aecia, appear on the underside of the leaf and eventually develop hairy, tube-like growths ... On the pome fruits, symptoms begin as small yellow or orange lesions, which eventually grow larger and turn brown.

diagnostic_features.value: Galls on juniper/redcedar always have small circular dimples across the surface, from which bright orange gelatinous telial horns (1/2 to 3/4 inch long) erupt in spring. On apple/crabapple leaves, yellow spots develop red rings around the edge, small raised black dots in the center on the upper surface, and produce hairy, finger-like aecia (less than 1/10 inch) on the leaf underside; on crabapple fruits the aecia give the fruits a spiky appearance.
diagnostic_features.quote: The galls, which are not always spherical and can have lumpy areas, always have small circular dimples across the surface (Figure 1A) ... large, gelatinous protrusions will erupt from the dimples along the surface of the gall ... The protrusions are typically bright orange ... These are called telial horns ... small chlorotic (yellow) spots, which eventually become orange and develop red rings around the edge ... aecia, appear on the underside of the leaf and eventually develop hairy, tube-like growths ... On crabapple this gives the little fruits a spiky appearance.

value: Hawthorn rust
Quince rust
Juniper broom rust
Japanese apple rust
look_alikes.quote: Although cedar-apple rust is the most well-known rust fungi, four different rust fungi cause similar diseases on the same types of trees in Minnesota.

value: Plant disease-resistant varieties of crabapple, apple, and juniper
Avoid planting eastern red cedar and juniper within several miles / a few hundred yards of susceptible Rosaceae plants
Inspect nearby juniper/red cedar trees in late winter or early spring and remove brown, woody galls before orange gelatinous structures form
Prune and remove infected twigs or branches on Rosaceae plants
Apply fungicides on the primary (juniper) host every 14 days from June to September

Apply fungicides on apple/crabapple beginning around flowering and continuing on a seven-day interval until telial horns dry up; sterol inhibitors (e.g., Myclobutanil) are most effective; copper and sulfur products can also be used
Spray trees/shrubs when flower buds first emerge until spring weather becomes consistently warm and dry
For organic apple production: bloom thinning combined with lime sulfur or a labelled biofungicide
Tolerate leaf spots and fruit infection on apples, hawthorns, serviceberry, and other Rosaceae
Avoid planting highly susceptible varieties such as Beacon and Wealthy apple where cedar-apple rust is a problem
treatments.quote: Tolerate leaf spots and fruit infection on apples, hawthorns, serviceberry and other plants in the Rosaceae family... Fungicides with the active ingredient Myclobutanil are most effective in preventing rust. Copper and sulfur products can be used as well.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: The disease is caused by the bacterium Erwinia amylovora

type_of_disease.quote: Fire blight is a common and very destructive bacterial disease of apples and pears

value: Foliar
Stem
Whole plant
Root
affected_parts.quote: the disease can kill blossoms, fruit, shoots, twigs, branches and entire trees

summary.value: Diseased blossoms become water-soaked, wilt and turn brown; blighted twigs bend at the growing point resembling a shepherd's crook; cankers form on bark with reddish brown sapwood beneath; fruit turn brown to black with bacterial ooze. Scorched appearance of leaves, blossoms, and young terminal shoots, with infected tissue turning black on pear and brown on apple.
summary.quote: Diseased blossoms become water-soaked, wilt and turn brown... As blighted shoots wilt, the twigs bend at the growing point and resemble a shepherd's crook or an upside down "J"

diagnostic_features.value: Blighted shoots wilt and the twigs bend at the growing point and resemble a shepherd's crook or an upside down "J"; during warm and humid weather infected shoots ooze droplets of creamy white to amber bacterial ooze; sapwood beneath a canker has a reddish brown appearance; infected tissue turns black on pear and brown on apple.
diagnostic_features.quote: the twigs bend at the growing point and resemble a shepherd's crook or an upside down "J" (Figure 4)... During warm and humid weather infected shoots will ooze droplets of creamy white bacteria... Sapwood beneath a canker has a reddish brown appearance

value: Select and plant resistant varieties
Prune out fire blight cankers and blighted twigs (cut 8-12 inches below visible infection)
Minimize summer pruning of blighted shoots; avoid heavy pruning in early summer
Follow proper pruning and fertilization practices; avoid excessive nitrogen and late-season fertilization
Make an early season application of copper
Spray streptomycin at bloom
Apply the plant growth regulator Apogee (prohexadione-calcium)
Control sucking insects
Sanitize tools with 10% bleach solution (1 part bleach : 9 parts water) between cuts
Use biological control product Serenade Garden Defense (Bacillus subtilis) as an alternative to streptomycin
Use hands to break out small blighted twigs rather than pruners
Remove cankers, blighted tissue, suckers, and root sprouts during the dormant season
Eradicate nearby fire blight hosts such as pyracantha, hawthorn, cotoneaster, and crabapple
Avoid excessive irrigation and avoid wetting foliage with sprinkler irrigation
Protect open blossoms during high-risk warm/wet weather
treatments.quote: Management Tactics for Fire Blight Select and plant resistant varieties... Prune out fire blight cankers and blighted twigs... Make an early season application of copper... Spray streptomycin at bloom... Apply the plant growth regulator Apogee... Control sucking insects

conflicts.note: USU restricts affected parts to leaves, blossoms, and shoots; OSU extends to whole tree mortality including roots.

deltas.reasoning: Multiple extension sources (PNW Handbooks, WSU Tree Fruit) explicitly describe water-soaked, dark/reddish streaking visible on/through the bark of fire-blighted apple stems, directly corroborating the observation.

web_support.quote: Cankers near the base of the trunk often appear dark, water-soaked, and purplish on the outside ... when the bark is removed, the cankered area may show red-brown streaking.
The bark of infected rootstocks may show water-soaking, purplish to black discoloration, and red-brown streaking may be apparent in the cambium just under the bark.
Trees develop reddish water soaked lesions on the bark.

deltas.canonical_says: Diseased blossoms become water-soaked, wilt and turn brown; cankers form on bark with reddish brown sapwood beneath; fruit turn brown to black with bacterial ooze.
deltas.image_shows: tan, chocolate-brown, and rust-orange
deltas.image_quote: The photo shows a blighted apple fruit with scorched leaves and stem. The affected tissue exhibits various colors.
deltas.reasoning: The tan/chocolate-brown/rust-orange palette is corroborated by multiple extension sources describing apple fire blight tissue as rusty brown to orange with tan ooze.

web_support.quote: Apple leaves and shoots turn a rusty brown color.
The systemically infected shoot has orange-colored leaves at the tip and is starting to wilt.
Rotted areas turn brown to black and become covered with droplets of whitish tan colored bacterial ooze.

deltas.canonical_says: (not specified)
deltas.image_shows: severe decline in the health of the plant
deltas.image_quote: The image shows a severe decline in the health of the plant, with brown and necrotic lesions on the fruit and leaves.
deltas.reasoning: Severe whole-plant decline with concurrent fruit and leaf necrosis is a well-documented advanced presentation of fire blight in apple.

web_support.quote: On apples and pears, the disease can kill blossoms, fruit, shoots, twigs, branches and entire trees.
Severe fire blight can cause trees to die, and fire blight bacteria overwinter as cankers in living tissue on the trunk and main branches and on mummified fruit.

deltas.canonical_says: Blighted twigs bend at the growing point resembling a shepherd's crook; cankers form on bark with reddish brown sapwood beneath
deltas.image_shows: a raised lesion
deltas.image_quote: The lesion is raised and located on the main stem.
deltas.reasoning: Early-stage fire blight cankers on apple stems are described as having raised/blistered margins before becoming sunken, providing partial support.

web_support.quote: the margin that may at first be indefinite or raised and blistered but becomes definite and marked by a crack or crevice later

dropped_contradictory.canonical_says: Blighted shoots wilt and the twigs bend at the growing point and resemble a shepherd's crook; during warm and humid weather infected shoots ooze droplets of creamy white to amber bacterial ooze
dropped_contradictory.image_shows: brownish galls
dropped_contradictory.image_quote: Galls are visible on the stem.
dropped_contradictory.reasoning: Fire blight produces sunken cankers and ooze, not gall-type swellings; gall-like growths on apple are associated with crown gall (Agrobacterium) or other pathogens, so this is inconsistent with the canonical pathogen.

web_support.quote: Cankers develop on trunks, limbs, and the graft union; are at first water soaked, later brownish red, and ultimately dark brown or black, with bark covering the cankered area appearing sunken, rougher and separating from injured tissue.
Bark on younger trees becomes water soaked and the cankers have a dark brown to purple color.

deltas.canonical_says: during warm and humid weather infected shoots ooze droplets of creamy white to amber bacterial ooze
deltas.image_shows: Dry branches with no signs of galls, blisters, scabs, exudates, or fruiting bodies.
deltas.image_quote: No abnormalities like galls, blisters, scabs, ooze, or fruiting bodies are visible.
deltas.reasoning: Bacterial ooze on fire-blight-infected shoots only forms under warm/humid conditions per NC State; its absence on dry tissue is plausible and not contradicted, but no source specifically corroborates this negative finding.

deltas.canonical_says: sapwood beneath a canker has a reddish brown appearance
deltas.image_shows: No distinct vascular streaking, dark blotches, or water-soaked streaks.
deltas.image_quote: The stem shows no distinct vascular streaking, dark blotches, or water-soaked streaks.
deltas.reasoning: Canonical reddish-brown discoloration is internal sapwood beneath cankers, not external surface streaking; the noted absence on the external stem surface is consistent with canonical but lacks direct external corroboration.

deltas.canonical_says: sapwood beneath a canker has a reddish brown appearance
deltas.image_shows: Water-soaked dark streaks extending from a lesion edge are visible through the translucent bark.
deltas.image_quote: Water-soaked dark streaking extending from a lesion edge are visible through the translucent bark.
deltas.reasoning: PNW Handbook and WSU Tree Fruit both document water-soaked bark with red-brown streaking visible from lesion margins, directly corroborating the observation.

web_support.quote: The bark of infected rootstocks may show water-soaking, purplish to black discoloration, cracking, or signs of bacterial ooze, with red-brown streaking apparent in the cambium just under the bark.
Bark on younger trees becomes water soaked and the cankers have a dark brown to purple color.

deltas.canonical_says: (not specified)
deltas.image_shows: White powdery-looking sporulation masses visible on the brown core.
deltas.image_quote: The white powdery spots on the brown core.
deltas.reasoning: Extension and peer-reviewed sources confirm that Penicillium expansum sporulation on apple is initially white before turning blue-green, so a white powdery mass on a decayed apple is consistent with early-stage blue mold sporulation.

web_support.quote: The rotted areas are soft, watery, and light brown, with older lesions covered by bluish green spores that initially are nearly snow white.
Fungal growth on the lesion surface, at first white, becomes pale blue as sporulation occurs.

deltas.canonical_says: (not specified)
deltas.image_shows: Concentric brown rings in the cut apple halves.
deltas.image_quote: The apple halves exhibit concentric brown rings.
deltas.reasoning: Peer-reviewed inoculation studies report that Penicillium expansum lesions on apple may exhibit concentric zones, so concentric brown rings are a recognized though not invariant feature of blue mold.

web_support.quote: Lesions were soft, watery, light to medium brown, with or without concentric zones, with smooth or irregular margins, and with blue green conidial tufts present mainly around the inoculation site.

deltas.canonical_says: (not specified)
deltas.image_shows: Two apple halves with concentric brown rings, indicating internal browning.
deltas.image_quote: The lesions display concentric rings with alternating light and dark colors.
deltas.reasoning: Internal browning with concentric rings on cut apple halves is plausible for advanced Penicillium expansum decay but is also a hallmark of bull's-eye rot, so the candidate is left provisional pending stronger evidence specific to blue mold cross-sections.

deltas.canonical_says: (not specified)

deltas.image_quote: The photo displays two apple halves with concentric brown rings, indicating internal browning.
deltas.reasoning: Blue mold flesh is typically described as light to medium brown rather than rust-orange; without external evidence for this specific color palette in blue mold, the observation is recorded as provisional.

deltas.canonical_says: (not specified)

deltas.image_quote: The image displays two cut apple halves with significant brown discoloration in the center, indicating the presence of disease.
